questions.questionText: The WHO definition of health (1948) describes it as a 'state of complete physical, mental, and social well-being and not merely the absence of disease or infirmity'. This concept is:
questions.optionD: Ecological
questions.explanation: It incorporates physical, mental, and social dimensions, rejecting the purely biomedical view.

questions.questionText: The 'Epidemiological Triad' model of disease causation includes:
questions.optionD: Host, Behavior, and Environment
questions.explanation: Disease results from complex interactions among these three core elements.

questions.questionText: The period between the entry of an infectious agent into the host and the appearance of the first clinical signs/symptoms is called the:
questions.optionD: Latent period
questions.explanation: During this time, the pathogen is multiplying but has not yet reached the threshold for symptoms.

questions.questionText: Primary prevention of a disease involves:
questions.optionA: Early diagnosis and prompt treatment to halt disease progress
questions.optionB: Actions taken prior to the onset of disease, which remove the possibility that the disease will ever occur
questions.optionD: Limiting disability after the disease is clinically manifest
questions.explanation: Immunization, sanitation, and health education are classic examples of primary prevention.

questions.questionText: Screening for cervical cancer using Pap smears is an example of which level of prevention?
questions.optionD: Secondary
questions.explanation: Screening aims to detect and treat early, asymptomatic disease (Secondary prevention).

questions.questionText: Primordial prevention, a relatively new concept, focuses on:
questions.optionB: Preventing the emergence or development of risk factors in population groups in which they have not yet appeared
questions.optionD: Specific protection against a known pathogen
questions.explanation: Example: National policies/education to discourage children from ever starting to smoke.

questions.questionText: The constant presence of a disease or infectious agent within a given geographic area or population group is termed:
questions.optionD: Sporadic
questions.explanation: Malaria is endemic in many parts of sub-Saharan Africa.

questions.questionText: An outbreak (epidemic) that affects a large proportion of the population over a wide geographic area (e.g., across continents) is a:
questions.optionD: Endemic
questions.explanation: The COVID-19 pandemic is a classic example of widespread global transmission.

questions.questionText: A 'carrier' of a disease is an infected person or animal that:
questions.optionA: Is immune to the disease and cannot transmit it
questions.optionB: Is currently suffering from the acute clinical symptoms of the disease
questions.optionC: Harbors a specific infectious agent in the absence of discernible clinical disease, serving as a potential source of infection
questions.optionD: Only transmits the disease through a biological vector (like a mosquito)
questions.explanation: Carriers (like Typhoid Mary) are critical in the transmission of many diseases.

questions.questionText: Herd immunity refers to the:
questions.optionA: Temporary immunity a newborn receives through maternal antibodies
questions.optionB: Innate genetic resistance of a specific ethnic group to a particular disease
questions.optionC: Short-term protection provided by the injection of pre-formed immunoglobulins
questions.optionD: Resistance of a group to invasion and spread of an infectious agent, based on the immunity of a high proportion of individual members
questions.explanation: When enough people are immune (e.g., via vaccines), transmission chains are broken, protecting the unimmunized.

questions.questionText: The 'Incidence' of a disease is defined as the:
questions.optionA: Number of deaths caused by the disease in a year
questions.optionB: Number of NEW cases of a specific disease occurring during a certain period in a population at risk
questions.optionC: Proportion of people with the disease who die from it
questions.optionD: Total number of ALL cases (old and new) of a specific disease existing at a given point in time
questions.explanation: Incidence measures the rate of occurrence of new cases (a measure of risk).

questions.questionText: The 'Prevalence' of a disease is defined as the:
questions.optionA: Number of NEW cases of a specific disease occurring during a certain period
questions.optionB: Total number of ALL cases (old and new) of a specific disease existing at a given point in time in a defined population
questions.optionC: Rate at which a disease spreads through a population
questions.optionD: Number of deaths per 1000 population per year
questions.explanation: Prevalence measures the burden of disease in a population at a specific 'snapshot' moment.

questions.questionText: In an epidemiological study, the measure of the strength of the association between a risk factor and a disease is typically the:
questions.optionD: Relative Risk (Risk Ratio)
questions.explanation: RR = Incidence in exposed / Incidence in non-exposed. RR > 1 indicates increased risk.

questions.questionText: Which study design is considered the 'gold standard' for evaluating the efficacy and safety of a new therapeutic intervention (e.g., a new drug)?
questions.optionD: Case-Control Study
questions.explanation: Randomization minimizes confounding bias, providing the strongest evidence of causality.

questions.questionText: A Case-Control study is best suited for investigating:
questions.optionA: Rare diseases or diseases with long latent periods
questions.optionB: The prevalence of a condition in a snapshot of time
questions.optionC: The incidence rate of a common infectious disease
questions.optionD: The natural history of a newly discovered virus
questions.explanation: By starting with cases, researchers can efficiently look backward for exposures without waiting years.

questions.questionText: A Cohort study typically starts by selecting groups based on their:
questions.optionA: Exposure status (exposed vs. unexposed) and follows them over time to measure disease incidence
questions.optionB: Geographic location only to measure current prevalence
questions.optionC: Randomization to a treatment or placebo group
questions.optionD: Disease status (cases vs. controls) and looks backward for past exposure
questions.explanation: Cohorts are followed prospectively (usually) to see who develops the outcome of interest.

questions.questionText: The 'Odds Ratio' is the primary measure of association calculated in which type of epidemiological study?
questions.optionD: Cross-Sectional Study
questions.explanation: Case-control studies cannot calculate true incidence, so they use the odds of exposure among cases vs. controls.

questions.questionText: In biostatistics, the 'p-value' represents the probability that:
questions.optionA: The study results are clinically significant
questions.optionC: The sample size was large enough to detect a difference
questions.optionD: The observed difference (or a more extreme one) occurred by random chance, assuming the null hypothesis is true
questions.explanation: A small p-value (typically <0.05) leads to rejecting the null hypothesis.

questions.questionText: Which measure of central tendency is most appropriate for a highly skewed (asymmetrical) dataset (e.g., household incomes)?
questions.optionD: Mode
questions.explanation: The median is robust against extreme outliers that heavily pull the mean.

questions.questionText: The Standard Deviation of a dataset is a measure of:
questions.optionA: The central or average value of the dataset
questions.optionB: The difference between the highest and lowest values
questions.optionC: The dispersion or spread of values around the mean
questions.optionD: The probability of a false positive result
questions.explanation: A larger standard deviation indicates greater variability in the data points.
